name: Arachne & Minerva — Ovid (Brookes More) — Illustrated (PD-art rotation)
description: Ovid's *Metamorphoses* VI.1–145 — the mortal weaver who out-weaves a goddess — in the public-domain Brookes More (1922) verse, LibriVox narration, word-by-word karaoke, with Baur's 1703 engraving of the transformation. A recursive-recording performance. Rotating public-domain artworks (~3s each), each anchored to the beat it depicts, credited on screen; the narration's word-by-word karaoke runs underneath. A recursive-recording performance.

metadata.audio_source: Google Cloud TTS (en-US-Neural2-D) — neural narration, AI-generated
metadata.translation_note:
## About this translation

This performance reads Ovid in the **Brookes More (1922)** verse translation — public domain in the US, and chosen because it *sings*: its blank verse carries aloud better than the archaic 1717 Garth/Dryden, while staying close to the Latin.

Every English Ovid is one translator's voice. The Latin original is ~2,000 years old; what you hear is More's 1922 ear for it. The lineage — Golding 1567 ("Shakespeare's Ovid") -> Sandys 1626 -> Garth/Dryden 1717 -> **Brookes More 1922** -> the modern flood — is part of the work, not a footnote.

This follows the repo's editorial stance: **carry the history; compost, don't cancel.** [Read the editorial principle](../EDITORIAL-PRINCIPLE.md)

[[placeholder]] A Portuguese legenda is planned. For Alice (Monteiro Lobato's beloved but racist translation) this "about this translation" page becomes a compost page — Nastacia speaking back.
metadata.content_note: Ovid's account includes Arachne's self-harm (she hangs herself before Minerva transforms her into a spider). The text is kept verbatim as public-domain Ovid; the illustration depicts the transformation, not the act. Named here, not erased.
